You will be in hell more than the classroom itself.

This class gave me gastritis. This professor switched up from last semester. No practice exams at all anymore. The midterm exams were monstruous. This was worse than CMSC250 with Fawzi. If Fawzi edges to proofs, then Sana Jahedi edges to stats.

day 1: "i read all of your planetterp reviews and this class is going to be really hard now"

Jahedi falls somewhere between a mediocre professor and a bad professor. Discussions, homeworks, and quizzes are all helpful and reasonable, however lectures are useless- the notes are useful but you are better off learning the content from the provided textbook.

What sours Jahedi’s class experience is her exams, and the attitude she expressed to us in response to our feedback. The first exam wasn’t unreasonable, I came in unprepared and was understandably challenged. The second exam however, was plain unfair. While all but one question apparently were “from homeworks and discussions,” the difficulty of those problems and content emphasized had a clear mismatch between the exam and practice content.

When presented with the fact that most students found the exam extremely difficult, Jahedi’s response largely came off as “Sorry, but you should have had no problem. I won’t consider adjusting the grading.” 

Granted, she already has extended margins for each letter grade (89%-100% nets you some type of A) and she’s within her rights to not adjust exam grades, the apparent attitude of “you guys are wrong that this exam was hard, it’s actually easy” feels incredibly disrespectful. 

I know she has read these reviews, so please consider re-evaluating your grading policy and how it might not reflect students’ actual understanding of course material. In my experience after four years here, if the resounding majority of the class says an exam is too hard, the exam was too hard.

This was supposed to be one of my easier classes this semester while taking CMSC330 and CMSC351 like most other students taking this class. WRONG. She was purposefully trying to make the class harder this semester. No practice exams or practice problems. When students asked for solutions to past quizzes, she refused to post them. Exam 2 was super unfair and pretty much every student could agree. All she told us was that it was easy and we should have done better. Despite the fact that her whole class is struggling, she continues to say there will be no curve.

Im not sure what happened over the course of winter break, but these reviews are not accurate anymore. do not take sana. 

there is ZERO practice exams or problems. she claims exams are straightforward and they are NOTHING of the sort. not only is she a confusing lecturer in class and I never understand the material, but the exams dont reflect what is taught. she refuses to curve or help us. no one thinks this class is easy. stat is supposed to be an easy class and suddenly im more worried about it than my cs classes. on an exam she said that there was a question with no possible information to solve and the answer was "to leave it blank".

I think Sana is the worst professor I’ve had so far at UMD. Sana read the past reviews on her class and decided to make her exams extremely difficult and provide no dedicated review materials for them in an effort to make us "learn" and not memorize. She simply tells us to study the lectures, discussions, and homework, but this makes it hard to gauge what the exams will be like since there’s a big gap between the difficulty of the homework and the lectures/discussions. 

She refuses to curve, despite low averages compared to previous semesters (in the 70s compared to 90 averages last sem), insisting her exams are "fair" and "easy" and that it is our fault we didn't do well.  Her lectures are often dry and confusing, and she’s been inconsistent with announcements. Never take Sana Jahedi for Stat400, unless you want to fail. I will preface this with the fact that Stat400's choice of professors isn't exactly wonderful. On the other side, you have Mestiyage, who is also good, but his lectures are the most boring thing ever.

Sana cannot teach Stat400 for her life. Her lectures are confusing. Very confusing. Confusing to the point where self-study is an option you are forced to take, not one that you choose. Everything is always our fault, especially when the class bombs a midterm, because she deems the class "easy" and the hell exams "fair". I'm assuming she had the same definition for the past couple of semesters, so she made it "slightly" less "easy and fair". Note that "slightly" is a very, very large misnomer. If she could, she'd set up a dunk tank and trap a student in there if they so much as answer a question wrong.
There is zero practice or provided material besides the homework, quizzes, and lecture material, in which I believe she would choose not to provide any if it wasn't required. Not to mention that she puts mistakes in answer keys (as another reviewer mentioned before), which doesn't help with studying at all and only serves to create more confusion within the students. 
On exams, if your answer is way too long and goes beyond the page, it's usually MORE right than if it stays within the page. This goes against what multiple other professors told me, which is that if you're taking 15 minutes to answer a question, that's probably wrong. For Sana, it's right.

Basically, if you had to choose between taking Sana for Stat400 or taking another year, you'd probably want to choose the latter. Save your grade and your sanity.
please bro dont take her. she was fair up until second exam and is being extremely unfair with the exam content. do not take it with her

At first, Sana looks like a decent enough prof. She seems nice enough and even says that STAT400 is not a class meant to vigorously teach statistics, so it's not too complicated. Plus, she doesn't even teach any R coding, so no unnecessary coding projects.

But then the problems start already on the first day.

Sana said that she wouldn't give us practice exams because, in the previous semester, students copied the answers straight from the practice when doing the exams without changing the numbers. This is a major issue already since previous semesters have an advantage due to having more prep material and she's screwing us over for something we didn't even have a part in. Second, she claims the exams she gives will be straightforward and that we'll do just fine by looking at the lecture and discussion notes plus the homework she gives. No more material to study from, just those three things.

This could not have been more false.

Exam 1 average: 29.61/39
Exam 1 median: 31/39

Exam 2 average: 30.27/50.5
Exam 2 median: 29.5/50.5

(Yes, the exam totals are nonsense. 50.5? Really?)

And after all of this and with so many students complaining, Sana said on Piazza: "There will be no curving to the grades, but people who have achieved extra credit throughout the semester, it will be implemented on their grades, at the end of semester."

This class is an absolute mess and the worst thing that could happen to anyone taking STAT for their requirements. Sana did not teach us the material well enough to learn how to properly implement it into our exams. Homework for the most part was fine, and quizzes were also not terrible, but the big part of the grade for the class, exams, are an absolute nightmare. She gives too many problems and expects you to instantly recognize the solution and write it down without second thought. What she calls "straightforward" could not be further from that word's definition than it already is. To top it all off, she won't even be generous enough to curve and became pretty defensive when the mention of it even came up. In the end, Sana proved she should not be teaching students if this is how she treats them after everything they've gone through and is not fit to be a professor.

AVOID TAKING SANA AT ALL COSTS.

She refused to provide practice materials for either quizzes or exams, and she explicitly declined to curve grades. Don’t take her class expecting an easy STAT4XX, especially if you're planning to take two CMSC courses that semester (as a CMSC student)

To keep it short, quizzes are easy and are derived from lecture/discussion material. Homeworks are EXTREMELY dumb VERY complex versions of lecture topics. The exams... Imagine being taught how to stand then tested on running. Final hasn't happened yet; I am praying for a miracle man.

I would assume that the review claiming the negative feedback is false is likely Sana’s burner account. Please understand that the negative reviews reflect genuine experiences, and when 99% of the class agrees on the same issues, it cannot simply be dismissed as untrue. If you actually read those reviews, you'll see that the main complaints focus on her failure to meet basic expectations as an instructor. Not providing practice materials and giving exams unrelated to the taught content are serious concerns and should not be considered acceptable. While some reviews may come across as disrespectful, keep in mind that this is a review section, people are sharing their honest thoughts and experiences. No one is using PlanetTerp to harass her; they are simply expressing what they went through. You can disagree with them, but you can’t just dismiss or invalidate their experiences, and none of this diminishes the credibility of the feedback provided.

while i didn't expect this class to be an easy A, i didn't expect it to be this unnecessarily difficult. i understand that she may feel jaded or smth from her experience last semester, but completely getting rid of practice material and making the exams completely unrelated to the lecture material just feels unfair. i don't understand how she expects us to learn and fully absorb the material from the very few examples she provides and very little practice. her attempt to make us "learn" only served to make this class unreasonably difficult.

She is evidently a much harsher professor now than she was the previous semester but nothing other than the second midterm was unreasonable in my opinion. She has 5 or so quizzes in the semester and although they aren't group quizzes, they were relatively simple and drops two of them. The homeworks are also not too difficult and she is very open to helping you work through them (also drops some of them). I think that most of these reviews are over the top. You can't go into a 400 level class expecting a free A when you aren't at the very least willing to put in the work for it. The only real critique I have is that the second exam's content was a bit too focused on the stuff we learning the week leading up to the exam and didn't have adequate practice with it, otherwise she does not seem any more difficult than the other professors for this class.

the absolute GOAT. Each week during discussion, he would go through some homework problems step by step and nicely answer any questions that people had. Was also very lenient with quizzes and would help us on those as well. Would highly recommend.
if you value not hating your life don’t take this professor. the best was i’ve heard someone describe this class is it’s like being taught to crawl and being tested on your ability to run a marathon.

Soyoung is an amazing TA for STAT400! She is so nice and always has a smile on her face. She explains concepts very clearly in discussion and cares genuinely about students' success. Highly recommend her!
Very great and understand professors. Gives you an hour after due dates to still turn in for full credit. Study guides are very helpful for the exam, and homework's and.R projects are all fair.

Okay outside of all of the reviews about how difficult this class is, this professor is just genuinely a liar. She said she would add credit to where it would benefit most, then went back on that, then halved the credit, and then just put it in the same place for everyone. I exceeded beyond what I thought I would need for the grade I wanted, but still will not get an A even after getting clarification as to how things were going to be graded multiple times (and guess what they weren’t graded that way). So disorganized and stress inducing.

She was definitely a lot stricter this semester (based on what I've heard from previous semesters), but I will say that she definitely cares about her students learning the content. She has extra office hours before exams, and she is really good about answering questions. She's also a pretty good lecturer. Her exams are generally fair. Her second exam was a bit tougher than her first (I didn't do too well on it), but looking back on it the questions weren't outrageous. Just make sure you actually understand how to do the problems, not just one specific problem. Most of the problems she gives on exams and quizzes are modified problems that she has already given before, so just make sure you understand the previous problems. Overall though, she is a solid professor, just don't expect it to be a cakewalk.

I want to start by saying that I think it is unfair for students to review a professor before the course has even ended. Sana is clearly knowledgeable in statistics and tries her best to convey information to students. I think one of the significant issues was that many students came in with the expectation that this class would be "free" because of her reviews from last semester. She was aware of this and tried to correct the learning expectations this time. This is where things became a bit of a mess. There were points in the semester when the homework assignments became very challenging. Lecture slides often had minimal information to understand the entire material, and we had limited review material. As a result, I had to spend a lot of time outside of class filling in learning gaps. However, I usually have to do a lot of outside learning, and it's kind of part of the college experience. Everything was pretty good except for our second midterm, which sparked outrage. The exam had too much depth for our time, making completing it fairly complex. Despite not curving the exam, she gave us ample opportunity to earn a higher grade with Piazza extra credit, problem extra credit, class weights, and the final exam. First, the quizzes and homework assignments make up 55% of our grade. Although I said the homework was consuming, I could always finish it. The quizzes were straight off the discussion worksheets and lecture examples. So this part of the grade was fairly easy to fortify. The final exam was based on the practice problems she gave us, and it was far easier than both midterms. She even dropped two quizzes. So while people are saying there was no curve, she had it all laid out to make their grades work out. I did average on the first exam and was terrible on the second exam, and am still ending with an A. She gave opportunities at the end and deserves recognition for that. Leaving reviews before you know your final grade is not helpful.

literally the worst prof I've had here, doesnt teach things relevant to homework and exams, his lectures are him in his lala land talking about either really simple concepts like how 1+1 is 2 or its him going into way too much depth with variables and terms nobody knows because he didn't teach them. Absolutely no instruction on homework and exam material. It's like he assumes you've taken this class before. WOULD NOT RECOMMEND. There's not even a textbook, there's a draft to the book he's writing that legit has an error on every other page it's comical how terrible this class was.

Sana is the physical embodiment of incompetence. She is not the evil person people make her out to be; she is just that bad at teaching and running a class.

1) She cannot explain anything for her life. If you asked her how to build a house, she would start all the way from when you chop down trees, go into excruciating detail about how to create planks, then just end with "and then you lay the foundation and build a frame" without further detail. She spends a ton of time going over unnecessary background/context, only to never explain the part that actually matters.

2) Some of her attempts to help students only end up harming them. As an opportunity for extra credit, she added "intentional mistakes" to answer keys for people to catch. That's questionable enough to begin with, but even worse, it gave her a loophole to avoid admitting when she's wrong. The key for one of the more difficult homeworks had a solution that was completely off base (like straight up not how you would approach the problem at all). When someone pointed this out, instead of her usual praise for catching the mistake, she just gave a dry response saying the solution wasn't completely typed.

3) She constantly switches up about grading policies. For example, after midterm 2, she provided some extra credit questions as a feeble attempt to make up for it (average was 30.27/50.5 and median was 29.5/50.5). Those questions went from being worth 10 points to 9 points to 4.5 points. She also originally said the points would be added to whatever helps your grade the most, later clarified the points could only be added to exams, then just added them to midterm 2 for everyone.

She does have a few redeeming points. The quizzes were similar to discussion worksheets, and she went from dropping your lowest one to lowest two. She also provided a practice exam for the final (average was 26.71/37.5 and median was 27.5/37.5). But given her pitfalls, that's just slapping a bandaid over a bullet wound.

I agree one of the reviews below, she is a liar. 
She kept changing things and was not transparent about the grading process. Midterm 2 was poorly received—the class average was around 60—and although she said there would be no curve, she promised an extra credit problem that could help improve our scores. The extra credit was worth 9 points, and she stated that it would be applied to whichever section (quizzes, homework, Midterm 1, or Midterm 2) would benefit our overall grade the most.

We assumed this would make a meaningful difference. However, after the final, she only applied 4.5 points from the extra credit, and applied it to Midterm 2 for everyone, regardless of whether that was the most beneficial section. In my case, applying it to Midterm 2 helped the least, and I ended up with an 88.88%—a B+. If the extra credit had been applied to any other section, I would have received an A-.

I emailed her about this before grades were submitted to Testudo, but received no response. After the grades were finalized, she replied that my grade was below the cutoff and announced that no grade changes would be made since grades had already been submitted.

Great professor, explains content well overall. Sometimes he rushes through examples but he's very receptive when you ask him to explain something again. Exams are super fair and homework is gone over in weekly discussions before due. Class can sometimes be a bit of a snoozefest but Danul is by far the best option for STAT400 and I would highly recommend him.

A solid professor overall.

It may not be as easy as STAT400 with Mestiyage from what I've heard, but nonetheless I think that Fernandes was a solid option for this class. Granted, his exams (particularly exam 2) can sometimes have some curveball questions, but they are generally related to what he teaches in class and especially related to the sample exams that he uploads, usually in a timely manner. I will say that his lectures were not the best, as his teaching style was a bit boring and he can sometimes be all over the place in his lectures, but they're not the worst and some office hours with the TAs will most likely clear up any confusion you have.

One thing to note though is that his final had some pretty hard questions, as about 50% of it is based on material from after Midterm 3, which is probably the hardest part of this course, which is the only reason he's getting 4 stars here and not 5. However, I would still recommend taking him as the rest of his class is generally not that bad.

Assignments are almost free 100's, so it's down to the exams, which aren't too bad if you know your calculus (and can be bothered to study). He provides an extremely generous study guide with answers, and makes both his lecture recordings and writings accessible. No quizzes too, which is really nice.

As generous as the assignments are I felt like Danul explained concepts in lecture in extremely roundabout and confusing ways, and I personally found it better to learn from the TA or the textbook as a primary source, but also useful to peek at the lecture notes and such secondarily.

As someone who did pretty poorly in this class, I think some of the reviews (especially ones from the middle of the semester) are slightly exaggerating because emotions were high and so people were reactionary. That being said, I don't think she's a particularly good professor (she has flaws) but she's not like the literal devil

The Bad:
- the course was kind of poorly organized, and I think that her disorganization and lack of transparency around it is what caused a LOT of the problems. The extra credit system is a pretty strong example of this - there were a variety of things you could get credit for but it wasn't always clear how much credit you were getting/whether you were getting it/how it would actually impact your grade. She also had a really confusing policy about dropped quizzes (she automatically dropped one but if you did poorly on quiz1 but well on quiz2 your quiz2 grade would replace your quiz1 grade and wouldn't count as the dropped quiz, but she also mentioned this being based on individual student evaluation?) which she then fixed by just dropping 2 quizzes for everyone and saying she intended to all along and just lied to motivate us. I don't know if she's lying about that but it's kind of mean to just lie about a course policy for the sake of "motivation"
- she gave out extra credit for Piazza but didn't really define what counted as a contribution worthy of getting points, so the STAT400 piazza was kind of a pain in the ass since everyone was just responding to things for the sake of getting points, even if they didn't know what was going on or have a good answer (sometimes students answered posts about logistics that instructors should have answered, sometimes you would get 4 people in the followups saying the same thing, or editing the student answer for the sake of getting more contributions, it was all very annoying)
- the rubrics for this class were bad, it was really hard to know what I actually lost points on (the graders overuse the point adjustment feature, and the rubrics themselves aren't the most clear)
- sometimes the answer keys had errors: apparently this was on purpose for the sake of the extra credit system but I think that's a bad choice. If someone catches the mistake and gets extra credit that's cool for them, but I imagine plenty of people just got more confused because they were looking at an answer key that was wrong, and they probably didn't learn the content correctly because they assumed the answer key was right
- she didn't let us see our final (like it wasn't on Gradescope) after it was graded which was annoying because I would have liked to know what I did wrong
- lecture wasn't super engaging (I will also admit fault here, I slept through some of them, they were at 9:30am in the armory) and I think she would probably be better at a different time (that wasn't so early), I think part of the issue is that she went pretty slowly through the set theory and combinatorics stuff, which made the first lectures really boring for anyone who kind of knew it, and also meant that when she went through the harder stuff, there was less time for it than there ideally would have been
- the canvas was organized poorly, it's just kind of hard to find things in the files there, which is annoying

The Good:
- she's not terrible at explaining things, and she's pretty patient. Sometimes this is to her detriment (she entertained way too many questions from people who probably should have just gone to office hours, it kind of disrupted the flow of lecture) but it was nice in lecture. She also lets people do the questions on the board which is fun! I like that
- her TAs were all really nice! I never learned any of their names except my TA's, but my TA was really nice, and when I asked for clarification from the other TAs during the exams, they were all very nice
- quizzes weren't too bad, they were usually pretty similar to the discussion practice sheets. The discussion practice sheets were also pretty helpful. I didn't do amazing on the quizzes but that's kind of a skill issue on my part
- She does post notes, which is nice
- The homeworks weren't easy but they weren't unreasonable imo, I could have done better on them if I didn't procrastinate so much, she does also drop some I believe

tl;dr - I don't think I would recommend her, and I don't think I would take a class with her again, but I also think a lot of these reviews make her seem way worse than she is. You don't need to like avoid her at all costs or anything. I think a lot of people expected this class to be easy because she made it super easy for the previous semester, and then people got upset when it was harder. I think this class was probably more challenging than it needed to be (Exam2 was bad for everyone) but I think she'll probably improve in the coming semesters

Overall good teaching. One big complaint though. Towards the end of the semester he all of a sudden announced some sort of down curve capping the Homework and Quiz average to a 92% in offer for the final replacing lowest midterm. I personally am not a fan of artificial down curves/capping and it was very unexpected. I wish he had announced this much earlier or did something that felt less forced. Only complaint but overall the class is taught very well with adequate resources provided to prepare for exams.

An awful professor. Her lectures have nothing to do with the homework and exams. Also, they are extremely monotone and boring. I have learned absolutely nothing from her course. After giving us an insanely hard midterm 2 with a 50 as an average, so many people got mad. Do you think she decided to apply curve? Nah she said that the problems were simple and we were just stupid. After seeing 30+ horrendous reviews on PlanetTerp, instead of curving, she decided to give us problems for the final in advance, and warned us that we would get the exact same ones on the real final, but with slightly adjusted values. The issue was that she did not give solutions for these problems (only for 4 out of 15) and the problems were insanely hard. Like they were really hard even to memorize because of how little we learned in this course and how complicated these problems were. I literally had no idea what was happening in these 4 solutions, the rest of the problems I had to ask ChatGPT or Google. I am just so mad about how bullshit this class turned out, and how I learned literally zero new information, and that I will not be able to use this class as a prerequisite, because honestly I cannot claim that i know something about statistics. In conclusion: awful, afwul, afwul + no curve. How is she allowed to teach at UMD I will never understand.

In case you're wondering, Danul and Mestiyage are two of the names for the same guy. 

Nothing brilliant, nothing flawed about STAT400 with Gunatilleka. He has a system figured out, and it worked for me. Safest/least risky choice. 

- 30% final, 2x15% midterm, 3x4% R Project, 28% (~8) homework. 
- Homeworks discussed in Discussion, people actually go surprisingly
- R projects mildly annoying, but like the homeworks, a grade booster
- Exams had slightly lower than typical averages, but nothing crazy. There were four versions of each exam, so it was pretty formulaic without curveballs. 
- Lectures recorded, handwritten notes, ~20% attendance. Many places to learn the content. He's a fine teacher, speaks clearly, etc, so I went, and that was enough. Just don't think too deeply at times. That could cost you your sanity. We don't spend much time on derivations, so turn your brain off and apply the formulas.

Ok this is specifically in response to all the genuinely spiteful reviews on here. I'm not here to judge who is and who isn't a liar or who is and who isn't incompetent. But it looks like this entire struggle's been brought on by the lack of communication between the Professor and the students. As someone who had Prof Jahedi the semester before last, I can confirm she's very easy to approach and talk with. That would help a lot with having the information you need to understand why the course is structured a certain way. Bashing the Professor like some of the reviews do below is a bit reprehensible when she's a human being. Think this entire thing is a miscommunication issue that can be avoided.

Jonathan is a fairly decent lecturer and a really good dude. However, I sometimes felt his lectures went a little fast or were difficult to follow. I suggest sitting close to the front if possible. The most useful thing is going to discussion. My TA, Amandeep, was the best I have ever had at my time at UMD across all classes I've taken. You really learn what you need to know in the homework, and just do the practice problems he gives for exams, and you'll do okay. I certainly could've gotten an A if I tried harder but I was more focused on not failing 351 and prioritized that unfortunately.
Amazing TA who would explain all steps in detail. Kind, flexible, and very receptive to questions.

Outside of the (outstanding) help from weekly discussions with my TA, I had to self-learn this course entirely.

My main gripe with this class is the lack of materials. There's a textbook that isn't particularly helpful, as it just goes over theory, and some videos that dictate that textbook. Outside of your TA, you're left with nothing to finish the weekly homework. Although he provides lots of practice questions for exams, they come with no answer keys or worked solutions, making them essentially useless. Even worse, Fernandes often promises worked-out solutions, but rarely delivers. When he did deliver, he had done only a few problems in the margins the night before the exam, some with glaring errors. Worked-out solutions are never given for the homework problems after they have been graded, so if you make a mistake, you're left to figure out how to solve it correctly on your own.

Although theory is taught, how to approach and complete problems similar to the ones on the homework, quizzes, and exams is not covered enough.

The saving grace of this class is that the exams are pretty easy and there are a ton of curves. If you're able to self-learn concepts well enough so that you can do any of the homework, quizzes, or practice problems, you'll end up with a 90+ on all midterms and the final. All homework and quizzes above a 92 were rounded to a 100, and the lowest midterm was replaced by the final if you did better on the final. The overall grade or final was also likely curved.

A pretty easy but somewhat challenging (in the sense that there is a lot of material) class. A lot of topics are covered (such as different distributions, estimators, probability, etc.) so just make sure you stay on top of the material. My only complaint is that I had 8 am discussion which was quite challenging to go to once a week especially since I am a commuter. I ended up missing almost half of the discussions, which sucked since there was a quiz every discussion. He records all lectures so I just watched those instead of attending lecture. He replaces your lowest exam grade with the grade you get on your final, and also curves the regular exams by 5 points and the final by 10 points (perhaps even more in certain semesters if the class is struggling). The HW were a bit time consuming but the TAs usually go over most of the answers during discussion. Without the TA's help it would usually take me 2-3 hours to finish otherwise. 

My only regret is the fact that I did pretty bad on two of the exams because I started studying too late. If I were to take this class again, I would start to review the material a few days prior to the exam rather than the day of. The final wasn't as bad as I thought (I got a 75% on it), just make sure you really understand estimators as most of the exam was based on that.

I guess in the fall Sana was too lenient and people didn't learn well, so she unfortunately way overcorrected this semester to the point where the class was unreasonably difficult. The lack of practice quizzes and exams made it very hard to study, especially because her somewhat arbitrary ordering and numbering of topics made it hard to use other books and resources.

However, Sana seems like a very kind person who genuinely just really wanted her students to learn and went about it the wrong way. I think that in time as she fine-tunes her teaching style she will be a really great professor.

Best TA I've had at Maryland. Always looked forward to his discussions. Explained everything step by step until everybody in the room was able to understand. Was kind, understanding, and overall exactly what you would want from a TA. Love this guy!

I really enjoyed this class! The coursework wasn't necessarily conceptually challenging, but there is a volume of it, as other reviews mention. Personally, I felt like Fernandes provided a lot of resources, such as posting notes, lectures, the textbook (which was provided, though rough) and his video lectures from 2020. Besides this, him and my TA were extremely accessible via office hours and email.

Before taking this class, a lot of reviews mention that he has a lot of R projects. The past semester, there was only one project, and I spoke with him about how it was pretty difficult as a non-coding intensive major. He understands, and he mentioned he's looking into other methods of visualizing the concepts so if the R projects are a concern, there's that.

In terms of other classwork, sometimes the homework feels a bit longer than necessary/tedious, specifically when it comes to listing out outcomes. Other than that, they felt pretty fair. Quizzes mirrored the main concepts in lecture, so as long as I paid attention, I did well. I also felt the exams were fair as well, never felt like there were any insane curveballs. Class averages were consistently high (90's range). My grade can only be attributed to my personal choices, and I definitely recommend him if you want a professor who cares about you succeeding.

One of the best TAs I've seen teaching. Took STAT400 over the summer taught by Mengting, not a hard class but concepts can be made too overcomplicated than needed but she explains most things very well. Very precise and put together class, one of the goats. Only nitpick is sometimes her accent makes certain words hard to understand and occasional errors while watching lectures/hw recordings confuses me, but she usually catches most of them in a few moments (we are only human after all). Solid teacher, better than most actual professors.

She taught really well. The exams were very fair and similar to what you'd find on the homework and practice exams. There are plenty of good resources to do well in this class. She uses the same material as most other stat400 profs.

For the summer session, the grade breakdown was 22% per exam (3 of them), and 34% for 6 homework assignments. She applies a very slight curve(5%) to each exam. Exam averages and medians were all above 90.

One of the GOATs here at UMD! As one of my very first professors here at College Park, I'm glad to have had Professor Gholampour for STAT 400 this summer. I'm still in the middle of the class, but I quite like it so far and want to post my review early. His lectures were every day, but he made them engaging and recorded the videos for us to review if we needed to study for the exams. There are also extra credit opportunities for struggling students, and the grading system is very lenient (whichever is lower: WebAssign homeworks or short quiz-like homeworks average, will be replaced by 0.9 times the difference; whichever is lower: midterm average or final average, will be replaced by 0.9 times the difference). Plus, the exams were pretty flexible, leniently graded (by a TA), and could be done from home.

I know this prof isn't at UMD anymore, but I decided a review might be useful for anyone who sees this in her future as a professor. I get all the positive reviews saying you really just have to try and talk to her, but that's the way she was the first semester. She was incredibly nice, and completely turned it around the second semester. I am not just saying that because it wasn't an easy A anymore. After talking with her, studying from past mistakes, taking notes and going beyond to understand content, and staying engaged in lectures—all things that should allow someone to do well in a course—it was still unreasonably difficult. 

Sana purposefully put questions that were of high difficulty, beyond what the course content really required (based on what other professors assessed who had been teaching for many more years). Her content was not aligned to any specific textbook, making it difficult to find additional practice problems that matched what she taught without lots of searching, as she provided no practice questions outside of the examples given in lecture. Also, during lecture, she'd spend a majority of the class talking about theory and derivations that we did not really need to know, while spending relatively little time on actually going over practice problems. Over an hour and 15 minutes, we could get through what another professor would've done in 20. She refused to curve, despite the averages being around 60% or lower (or around that, it's been a semester). Additionally, though she was nice during office hours sometimes, she was not a nice person. Her TAs, who were very helpful throughout the semester in discussions and supportive whenever we needed it, she accused of helping students cheat because they weren't collecting exams quickly enough in a 100+ person class where they needed to organize exams at the same time as receiving them. 

She promised extra credit to offset the poor performance in Exam 2, arguing that we should earn the extra points rather than be given a curve. She told us repeatedly that this would be applied to whichever assignment category this benefitted us the most, and if that were true, I would've earned an A. She ended up saying that was too complicated and just gave us a tiny amount of extra credit (the points we earned for extra credit were divided so that the grade increase wasn't too much) in an assignment category that affected our grades much less, relatively. 

Overall, unprofessional, petty, and seemed much more annoyed at having to teach than wanting to. If you see this in the future and have the opportunity to take a course she teaches, you should hope that she has grown as a person. I completely understand being annoyed at students taking advantage of your kindness in the first semester that you taught, but that is not a reason to be unfair to students the next semester. The second star is for recognizing that she did at least try to support us at some times, even though she did not do well at it.

DONT TAKE HIM. THE SEMESTER HAS BARELY STARTED AND I CANT EXPRESS HOW MUCH I REGRET NOT SWITCHING OUT OF THIS SECTION. THE HOMEWORK DOES NOT REFLECT ANYTHING WE DO IN CLASS. PEOPLE WALK OUT OF HIS LECTURES BECAUSE HES JUST WASTING EVERYONES TIME. PLEASE DONT TAKE HIM
Literally the GOAT. His OH and discussion got me through the course.

Given that we are only 3 weeks into Zheng's first semester of teaching, I'm only giving 4 stars just to balance out the review below. This is by no means a complete review, though it doesn't immediately justify giving someone a 1-star for that reason.

The lectures are loosely based on a drafted version of lecture notes from Jonathan Fernandes and Mestiyage Gunatilleka, both of whom are historically high-regarded professors for STAT400. There are gaps of explanation during the lecture; some parts feel glossed over, likely unintentional due to the nature of reading off the draft notes. There have also been some observable moments of language barrier, such as reading "Independent Trails" instead of "Independent Trials", or some handwriting issues mid-lecture that students occasionally have to raise their hand to question what was written, or correct the professor in writing P(B|A) instead of P(A|B).

According to Zheng, if he had to put a number on the difficulty of the coursework, he mentioned the following towards the end of the first lecture:
"Homeworks are... like an 8/10"
"Exams are, 5/10"
"The quizzes, these are like 2/10"
"The homeworks are by far harder than anything you will see on the exam"

As of now, we've only had one homework (no homework was assigned during the first 2 weeks). There were four problems, each one reflecting what we learned in class: basic combinatorics, binomial trials, geometric probability, and the inclusion-exclusion principle. They do not expect you to know the answer just from the lectures alone - it is explicitly encouraged to use outside material (internet included) when completing it.

The first midterm isn't till October, the second one isn't till November, and the cumulative final is in December. I cannot speak for how the exams go until then. Whether you choose to go to the lectures is up to your learning style: Are you actually incentivized to study outside of class? Or would you do better with a primer in-person?

Until then, take the preceding reviews with a grain of salt.

A bit of context, this is now a post-semester review being written roughly a week after final grades locked in. I already left a mid-semester review, where I gave Zheng only 4 stars to balance out the immediate 1-star review that someone left only 3 weeks into the semester. Now giving my afterthoughts:

As the more recent reviews have already said, Zheng definitely improved over the semester, so I won’t restate everything they covered. I mainly want to give some context for why the early reviews were so harsh.

After Midterm 1 (with an average of ~76), the GroupMe turned into an echo chamber where a vocal group of students circulated the same complaints for weeks. One of those students made a Google doc listing the issues with Zheng, and people in the chat were explicitly encouraging others to leave 1-star PlanetTerp reviews based on these complaints. That doc later got cleaned up into a printed letter addressed directly to Zheng, asking whether he could provide extra credit, more practice problems, drop the lowest midterms, etc. This letter was then left at the back of class for students to sign if they wanted to.

To be fair, I’m not sure Zheng would have become as lenient as he did if that letter hadn’t happened, so some of that pushback was probably warranted, even if it shouldn’t take something like that for changes to happen in a class.

Personally, I thought the Midterm 1 practice was definitely enough. I skipped a few problems that clearly weren’t exam-relevant, but most of them lined up with lecture examples or homework. Some questions were basically the same concept with different numbers. I get why people were frustrated, but a lot of the “nothing like the practice” claims were overstated. For Midterm 2 and the final, the practice was even closer to the exams; arguably too close in some cases.

Zheng's sections were originally meant to be taught by Anurag Dey, who is known for being an "easy A" according to his PlanetTerp reviews for STAT401. We got switched to Zheng about a month before the fall semester started (i.e., during summer break), so it's possible that the same people were expecting that kind of class and were frustrated when the difficulty didn't match those expectations.

I agree with another recent review that 3.5/5 is probably a more accurate rating, but I’m giving a 4 just to round up and reflect the improvement and to help improve the average. Zheng has shown he has the capacity to improve, communicates fairly well given the language barrier, and is certainly not the "worst" choice for STAT400 as other reviewers had initially put it.

In the second half of the semester, he got so much better. His midterm 2 was really good and he gave us a practice exam that was very similar to the actual exam. He also told us exactly what kind of problems would be on the exam. His final exam was extremely easy, it was almost a copy and paste of the practice exam he gave us before the final exam. It was his first time teaching so the first half of the semester was rough, but he listened to the students advice and really turned things around. The bad reviews were mostly all from the first half of the semester after the first midterm which went horribly.

Hatice is very nice, I like her. But it's always very difficult to stay engaged during lecture, in part due to her style. This definitely isn't the hardest course in the world, and has a decent enough curve, but the exams were definitely some of the harder things I've taken.

I had him for fall 2025
Lectures are pretty boring, he mostly just reads from the textbook and stumbles over his words frequently, so its a good idea to have the textbook open while attending. 
He didn't collect homework this semester, but had frequent quizzes which were pretty much rehashed homework questions. As long as you do the homework, you'll be fine for the quizzes.
Midterm exams were fair, but he threw a curveball for the final making it much harder than it probably should have. People below mentioned what he did so I'm not gonna repeat it here. At least he gave us a nice curve to compensate for it.

tldr: If you have no other options, he's fine, but I wouldn't be jumping at the opportunity to take him again.

I was a little bit harsh in my last review. but you have to go to lecture, simply looking over her posted notes is not enough because they are often missing important things she covered during lecture/on exams. She's nice though, just not organized on Canvas.

He's a fine, default professor I'd say. His teaching is really straightforward and simple. He doesn't go too easy or too hard and his exams are really fair (EXCEPT THE FINAL) and the quizzes are trivial when you have a TA that basically gives you the answers beforehand. He curved everyone's grades insanely hard after everyone bombed the final he told us not to worry about so I really appreciate him for that because I KNOW I didn't earn this A, lol. People say he's boring but I don't think so. He teaches what's in the book and his exams don't throw curveballs. If he says "you'll have to do something like this on the exam" then chances are that exact question is word-for-word on the exam. All of this is an exception from the final literally do not trust what he says about the final because he straight up LIED to us about it. And then curved my B- into an A somehow, so.. I don't mind him too much. Soft recommend.

name: STAT400
title: Applied Probability and Statistics I
description: Prerequisite: 1 course with a minimum grade of C- from (MATH131, MATH141); or students who have taken courses with comparable content may contact the department. Credit only granted for: BMGT231, ENEE324, or STAT400. Additional information: Not acceptable toward graduate degrees in MATH/STAT/AMSC. Random variables, standard distributions, moments, law of large numbers and central limit theorem. Sampling methods, estimation of parameters, testing of hypotheses.
